sentences.text: Emanuel Mittelman, E-M-A-N-U-E-L M-I-T-T-E-L-M-A-N.
Michalovce, M-I-C-H-A-L-O-V-C-E.
In eastern Slovakia.
January the 5th, 1925.
I will be, January the 5th, 72.
My hometown was a city of approximately 25,000 inhabitants, or so I think.
[PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] There were about 5,000 Jewish people.
Mostly of them were religious.
The city was small town-ish, meaning nothing sophisticated as far as buildings, and so forth.
The Jewish community maintained many, many synagogues in order to please different segments of the Jewish population.
They had places where every segment of the population felt more at home-- so there were more modern people, there were more-- but everybody was orthodox.
The government forced each and every one to be a member of the Jewish community, even though you were not forced to go for services.

[PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] I would say that most of the population were on the-- of on a scale of one to 10, as far as finances and income, probably 30%, 40% were on the number four, if one would be the lowest in income.
And then you had maybe 2% that were high income, and the rest of them were like middle class.
And we belonged to, I would say, number four.
Most of us in the city were living among our-- our own.
In other words, there was no such thing as a ghetto, of course.
But we were in-- I would say, to a large extent, Jewish people kept together.
Even though we did business with the non-Jewish world, but we had no non-Jewish friends.
Everybody was friends with his own or her own kind.
And I would say there was like a-- like a unmade wall between the Jewish population and the non-Jewish population.
Schooling, since we had no yeshivas, as they are known today, we had two kinds of schools.
We had the cheder, which was the Jewish school.
And when you were a little bit bigger, you used to go to a yeshiva, was the higher-- higher learning establishments.
And the other ones, the other half a day, was public schools.
And it was obligatory, of course.
And for eight years, everybody had to attend public schools.
So we went together, boys and girls, Jews and non-Jews.
And it wasn't very pleasant for the Jewish person to attend a mixed school, since they were all Catholic, of course.
And Jewish people, especially the ones who were in their appearance different than the modern Jew or the non-Jew, were usually mocked and laughed at.
And a lot of times, also subjects to beatings by the non-Jewish peers.
How about you, personally?
Did you experience anything like that?
I have experienced, many, many times, abuse in-- in-- in-- in the school.
Especially by-- when we had these obligatory prayers in the morning, school prayers.
And in every class there was a crucifix.
And of course, we were excused from praying, or-- or-- or saying something.
Nevertheless, the majority of the kids in the class were always mocking us for not keeping up with them.

And we also had, of course, teachers who were so anti-Semitic-- personally, many of my friends who had side curls-- which are called in Jewish, payots-- were a lot of times pulled by the teachers as a punishment.
And I don't have to tell you what pain that causes.
So we were subjected to abuse because of our Jewishness, without any doubt.
My father was a very, very strict Orthodox Jew.
I would even call him fanatic to an extent.
And my mother was also coming from a very, very religious family.
And in general, we were very observant at home, daily services, and were always, you know, had to attend to, under strict rules.
And of course, when we were little, we had to have these services in class.
But I would say the house was kept very, very strictly, even to my today's way of thinking, in an orthodox manner.
There were no deviations whatsoever to-- from-- from religion.
And it wasn't really forced upon you.
You-- you grew up with it.
I mean, that was our way of living.
Examples as what?
Well, let's put it this way.
There's without any doubt that everything in the house was kosher, because we did not enjoy the-- the pleasantries that we can enjoy today, as far as the bounty full of foods and so forth.
So we lived on a minimum.
But nobody went hungry to bed.
As far as the strictness of the religion was that you had very, very little-- a minimum time-- in the day for playing.
And most of it was pushed by my father, especially, towards Jewish studies, to extents that, in today's world, would be considered very, very extreme.
Even in the most extreme neighborhoods, it would be considered-- this was very fanatic to-- I give you an example.
Like when I had a free minute, I had to go-- instead of playing-- I had to go to Hebrew school for Hebrew studies, and so did my brothers.
My father was-- at last, when we left home, at least-- he was a wine merchant.
And we were making wine wholesales, selling it wholesale and retail.
Kosher wine and non-kosher wine.
Yes, I had-- I had-- altogether, we were seven-- two sisters, five brothers.

In our city, I would say things began changing approximately 1939, 1940, when the Slovakian Republic came out with various laws that influenced the Jewish populations, restrictions such as Jewish people were not allowed to walk on the street after 6 o'clock, such as wearing a star or a band, such as Jewish people were now allowed to have only two suits-- limit of two, and various restrictions.
And they came out, these laws, were repeated-- repeated very often.
Every time, they come out with something new that one cannot have-- restriction-- restricting in banking, restricting in how much money one can own, and so forth.
I was kind of young, of course, when-- when the era of that you're questioning me right now, was when I was approximately 14 years old.
And our youth were not as sophisticated as American youth is today, with the television and media and so forth.
The first time I heard about Hitler is, I remember when the newspaper came out with the headline that Hitler has occupied Austria.
That's the first time that I realized who's Hitler, and what's Hitler, and-- and we're start talking about it.
Not favorably.

As a matter of fact, we had many people who were working for the government, like policeman and-- and officials that were in various government positions, used to come to us at least once a year around Christmas time, and my father would fill up their bottles with wine as presents, as gifts, of course.

And-- and these very people, who knew us the closest-- we were not intimate with those people, but they used to come in and out-- they were the first people that-- that would come in after a certain law was passed by the government, the Jews are restricted to one item on another, they would walk in, the same people would walk in, and say, OK, Mittelman, I know that you have more than two suits.

No, and for the simple reason, because by now, probably it's no secret that nobody in his right thinking mind would come up with-- with results or fears that we know now that came out, out of all that.
So nobody even talked about-- about leaving.
Not really.
I was-- I was-- I was so childish in my mind at 14, 15, that I wasn't looking forward.
There was no such a thing as a goal in my mind that someday I'm going to be such and such.
Too playful.
Yes, I was a student.
When that all began, I was only studying in Hebrew.
Oh, by the time it all started, I was already out of the education, public schools.
So there was no more subject matter to me at all.
But my sister, who was younger than I, they were forced out of the schools.
Next, then, I attended various higher education schools out of town.
One of them was in Humenne, which is not far away where I come from.
And one of them was in Hanusovce, which is near Presov, which is also not too far away with a bus, maybe an hour's ride, at that-- at the speed we used to maintain at that time.
Today probably would be half an hour.
And I was then in Nitra, which is a larger city, not far away from Bratislava, from the capital.
These three places I attended at various times.
But there was pressure, intenseness, felt at all times.
There were-- there were-- people were starting to crack around jokes, and tell-- tell-- tell what they call today Nazi jokes.
They used to use them more as the-- make the jokes with the-- with the guard, the Hlinka Guard, that we had in Slovakia, which was an equivalent with the Nazi SS guards.
And we used to, in order to keep up our spirits, so to say, we developed kind of a joking-- not to be dehumanized, so to say, by all that what's happening in our very little mind.
Don't forget I was-- I was only 14, 15, 16 at that time.
And whatever was going on in my mind, what the world meant, we were trying to overcome the adversity by creating some jokes, and some fun, and some laughter, and so forth.
By the time I was-- by the time I was about 17 years old, the-- the talk in town was already that-- that the Slovaks want to gather the young girls, and the young men to send them to Poland.
That was-- that's what they said for a couple months, for forced labor.
And after which, we will come back, and everybody will have, so say, fulfilled his norm.
And there was a lot of talk about it, because nothing in writing until it really started around February-- end of February, beginning of March in 1942, when it became effectuated.
And they really started to taking the young girls first-- which-- who they shipped off, of course, to-- to a certain gathering camp, to Zilina.
And from there on, they shipped them to either Auschwitz or any other camps.
Yes, we had-- we had, as a matter of fact, in the-- when the occupation of Poland happened, we had several times a-- a few men who ran away from Poland.
And one of them, I remember-- I can't remember his name-- but I remember clearly that he walked for weeks over the fields, hiding.
And by the time he arrived in our city, he had no teeth, because he-- he-- he was like on-- like on a-- a starvation time, eating just what he found in the fields, and so forth.
By the time he arrived, he had almost no teeth left.
And I remember we took him into our home for-- for a period of time, and-- and I can't recall exactly later on what happened with that person.
But to the best of my knowledge, I think he was deported with the rest of us from Slovakia to concentration camps.
Yes, he did.
But you have to remember that, at that time, the only thing that would happen was that there was mass shootings, mass killings.

There was no-- there was no such a thing as-- as-- he did not know any information about-- that he could give us, so we would know, wait a minute, be cautious, because you're going to fall into the same trap.
So it was all just that-- that there is-- the Germans are mean to the Jewish people, that they're killing them.
And they're-- they don't take any kind of exception if it's a man, woman, child, old people, young people, so forth.
As a matter of fact, it's an interesting question, because as I mentioned before, we were not really in a-- in a classification what you'd call a middle class, or a solid middle class family.
When Hitler occupied Poland, the Nazis-- or the-- I should say the German soldiers-- needed food and-- and they needed wine.
I don't have to tell you, on the fronts, they tried to give the soldiers the best they can.
So some German officers would come in, in our city.
And I remember the first time when they walked in.
It was on a Sabbath.
They walked into my father's house, and they were very polite.
And they told my father that they would like to buy some wine that they need for the soldiers on the front in Poland.
We were not occupied yet, at that time, of course.
I'm talking about '39, '40.
And my father told them, I'm sorry, I cannot deal with you today, it's the Sabbath.
And-- and-- and no matter how much money you want to spend, there's no doubt about it that I will not even discuss it with you.
If you're willing to wait till the evening, when the Sabbath is over, then we can talk business.
And they excused themselves.
They were very polite.
I can remember this like today.
They left.
They were from the Wehrmacht, you know, the regular army.
And they came back in the evening, and-- and I recall very vividly that they came in with some briefcases.
They bought our whole wine cellar.
I mean, every little bit that was there, lock, stock, and barrel, so to say.
They took out their-- opened up their suitcases, and they had brand-new, printed Slovakian money.
And they paid everything in cash, no matter what the price was that my father asked, no bargaining, nothing.
And from this time on, for some reason-- it must be upon the misery of the other countries-- we started to do much better than we ever did before.
Because the minute my father got that money, we ran right away and we replenished our stock, and so forth.
And they kept coming back every few weeks, and so forth, and buying the whole thing over and over.
And it became such a-- a interesting deal, that we started believing that from now on we were going to be rich.
So we had a stable in our, like, backyard where, when I was younger, we used to have a couple of cows and a horse, and so forth, which was discontinued by now.
So we converted this to-- if you can picture-- a little automatic bottling of wine place.
And we installed some oven there, because we had to cook the wine, in order to pasteurize it, and so it shouldn't spoil.
And we started bottling wine now, because we knew that the Germans will come, with [INAUDIBLE] labels.
And we really felt good about, for the first time in our life-- little did we know that it-- that this is all happening on the misery of others.
But that's-- that's what happened.
That we have now something to look forward-- we're really going to become upper middle class people, because things were going real well.
When the Germans came in?
We spoke German.
My father and I, all our whole family, we spoke fluently German.
In general?
Well, in school, when I went to public school, I speak-- I spoke Slovakian.
With my parents at home, we spoke like half Yiddish and half German.
And I know, when I was in the yeshiva, in the higher school of learning, our correspondence with my parents were in German.
It was in Jewish letters.
The alphabet was Jewish, but the pronunciation was German.

I mean, technically, I would say, living in the Czechoslovakian Republic, that we probably belonged to the German minority-- sounds very laughing, I mean, even to state this-- but we belonged to the German minority in-- in-- in the Slovakia city of Michalovce.
I even went-- I even took German in school.

1942, the first thing that happened in the middle of March, approximately, when they were starting to gather and take away girls, a lot of people sent their young daughters-- talking about 18, 19, 20, 21, 22-- to Hungary, of course illegally.
Because in Hungary, the Jewish people were not touched yet, officially.
So in order to escape being taken away by the Slovaks and then delivered to the Germans, my father decided that he's going to send my-- my oldest sister, who is today in Israel, to Hungary.
So we found some connection.
And for money, of course-- we were supposed to, my two brothers were supposed to go with her, which they in fact did, to a neighboring city of Trebisov.
And there they paid a guy who was supposed to smuggle her across the border.
Except that this guy took the money, and he turned around and notified the police.
And both my two brothers and my sister were captured by the police, and put on a train and brought back to our hometown.

And I remember, as vividly as today, my father was ill at that time in bed, and he asked me I should board the train to Trebisov, to that city where that happened, and try to sit in the back of the train to see what's going to happen with my two brothers, with my sister.
Somehow they got-- we got notified that they were captured.
And I was, as a matter of fact, on that train as they brought them back to Michalovce.

And they were at that time still a little bit ashamed for the public, so they did not have a march from the train station through the front of the city, rather than through a back way towards the jail.
And I was at a distance of about maybe 50 or 100 feet, trailing them so I should be able to tell my parents that's-- what's happening.
And they brought them to a place where the jail was.
And of, course, they all locked them up.
And I ran home quickly to tell my parents what I saw.
And my father tried, through different channels, through people that he knew during all the all years that he was there, to try to get them released.
But it was of no avail.
It's towards, the-- I would say towards the end of March-- maybe about, I would say around the 20th, or so-- they took them away from jail, both my two brothers and my sister.
And they carried them away, I did not know where-- at least for awhile.
And they disappeared, period.

It was only about a week after that happened, when they left town, that one evening I was told by a cousin of mine, that I used to frequent-- you have to remember that this is a time already where people felt the pressure, and all we were able to do is sit together and-- and-- and talk about plans.
What are we going to do?
The future was so questionable, that we really-- nobody was smart enough to come up with an idea, with a solution.
What do we do in a case like this?
I mean, nobody was really-- I don't know if the word is sophisticated, to-- to do-- what are we-- what are we doing?
Are we running?
And-- and if we are running, where are we running?
Is there a place we have to run?
Is there a way how to save yourself?
What-- what are we doing?
So that was going on, all the discussions.
And I was sitting with my cousin.
He was a little older, and he told me that there's a rumor in town that tonight will be the first night where they're going to pick up the young boys.
And they're going to take them to forced labor.
And as I mentioned before, after 6 o'clock, Jewish people were not allowed to walk in the street.
But I was always kind of a wild one.
I always took chances.
And I wasn't really afraid, because when you're young, you-- you gamble your life.
You're not worried so much.
So I remember, I came home around maybe 7 o'clock for that discussion with my cousin.
And I had some supper, and I went straight to bed.
It was really early.
I remember this like today.
And I fell asleep very quickly.

And by about 8 o'clock-- I didn't know at that time what time it was, sounded to me like-- like it's midnight-- around 8 o'clock, I had two flashlights shining in my face, and some guys yelling, get up!
Get up!
Get up!
In Slovakish.
They were guys who came to take me away.
And in the commotion, my mother, of course, was-- was crying.
Of course, maybe-- maybe it's better, yet today, that-- that she didn't know what-- where I'm heading.
So the thought was-- and that's what they convinced us, even the Jewish leaders, that it's a question of going away for a couple of months to forced labor.
And personally, I really wasn't afraid of a couple of months of forced labor, because I was a kind of a guy-- I thought, I'll survive a couple of months of forced labor.
It's not the worst thing in the world.
I mean, we had-- we had, before, they used to take Jews already a year, two years before.
We used to have in winter a lot of snow, they would take Jewish people to-- to shovel the snow, for days-- days in, days out.
And nobody gets killed from a little work.
It's not the most pleasant thing that you're being subjugated to and picked just because you're Jewish.
But we thought, for survival purposes, we'll do it.
And they took me away that evening, and the-- the two fellows who woke me up first-- I was the youngest-- took me along the street [INAUDIBLE].
We went from house to house, and they had a list whom to take.
And then they deposited us to a gathering place, which was a public school, really.
